topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

Maya stared at the printed email, the words blurring into an indecipherable smudge on the screen. Her coffee, forgotten on the cluttered desk, had gone cold, the lukewarm liquid mirroring the sudden, icy stillness in her chest. "Moving?" she managed to croak, the sound thin and reedy. She reread the sentence three times, her thumb tracing the familiar name on the screen. Across the country. It wasn't a joke; it was a finalized decision, a ticket punched for a life that suddenly felt impossibly distant. Her hands started to tremble, a slight, uncontrollable vibration that made the cheap plastic pen rattle in the holder. She pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. She needed air, needed to process the sheer scale of the news, but every breath felt shallow, as if her lungs had suddenly forgotten how to expand fully.

[story 2]

Liam picked up his phone, his fingers hovering over the contact labeled 'Chloe.' He looked down at the notification—a confirmation of her relocation schedule. The room seemed to tilt slightly, the familiar posters on the wall suddenly mocking him with their unchanging presence. He let out a slow, ragged exhale, the sound escaping him as a low, involuntary groan. He walked to the window, pulling the blinds open just a crack, letting the bright afternoon sun flood the room, which only seemed to intensify the dizzying reality of the situation. He watched a leaf drift slowly down the oak tree, his gaze unfocused, unable to anchor itself to anything solid. "Wait," he whispered, the word catching in his throat, sounding foreign even to his own ears. He squeezed his eyes shut, trying to physically hold the pieces of the announcement together, but they kept scattering into a million sharp, disconnected shards.

[story 3]

Sarah found herself leaning against the kitchen counter, gripping the edge so tightly her knuckles were white islands against her skin. The news, delivered via a text message, had landed with the force of a physical blow. She didn't move for a full minute, just absorbing the facts: new city, new job, new continent. A strange, hollow sensation bloomed in her stomach, a nauseating lightness that made her want to sink into the tile floor. She looked down at her hands, noticing the faint tremor running through her fingers, a subtle, unsettling quiver she hadn't felt before. When she finally spoke, her voice was surprisingly steady, though pitched unnaturally high. "So... you're really doing this?" The question hung in the air, heavy and awkward, a testament to the sheer absurdity of the situation crashing down around her.
